exemplar.title: Investigating the effect of damping on the motion of a bicycle during speed wobble

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the effect of damping on the motion of a bicycle during speed wobble

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Logical progression from introduction through theory, modelling, analysis, and conclusion
Systematic use of section headings and embedded figures aiding navigation
weaknesses: Occasional digressions and narrative bulk reduce conciseness
Duplicate section headers and missing equation numbering hinder cross-referencing
Minor algebra slips and abrupt unit changes interrupt flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall, the exploration is coherent and logically developed, with clear structure and mostly relevant content. Minor issues with repetition, formatting, and conciseness prevent it from reaching the top band, so a score of 3 accurately reflects the consistent but not exemplary level of presentation.

strengths: Consistent and mostly accurate use of mathematical language and notation (variables defined and applied)
Effective use of multiple representations (derivations, graphs, schematics) to support understanding
Clear logical structure linking mathematical steps to physical interpretation
weaknesses: Occasional notation slips (e.g., repeating râ twice, unit ambiguities)
Missing axis labels and equation numbering in graphs diminish reproducibility
Some algebraic steps could be made more transparent with intermediate explanations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although there are minor errors and opportunities for clearer labelling, the communication is generally very clear, with effective multi-modal representation and consistent notation. This places the work in band 4 for Mathematical Communication.

strengths: Strong personal narrative framing the problem around the studentâs own cycling incident
Clear choice of real-world parameters (turning radius, road length) showing personal approach
Demonstrates independent thinking in selecting and measuring scenario-specific data
weaknesses: Model framework remains largely conventional with limited novel adaptation
Simplistic assumptions (constant damping coefficient, no rider input) reduce depth of personal insight
Creativity confined to parameter choice rather than methodological innovation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration shows clear personal engagement and genuine independent thinking, but the modelling approach follows standard templates without exceptional innovation, fitting band 2 for Personal Engagement.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on choice of assumptions and their impact on outcomes
Thoughtful analysis of results in terms of forces, rider capability, and safety implications
Clear evaluation of limitations and proposals for more realistic modelling enhancements
weaknesses: Depth of reflection limited by lack of quantitative sensitivity or alternative scenario testing
Critical analysis of mathematical errors and limitations remains brief
No implementation of alternative models or data sets to extend evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is present and meaningful, with thoughtful discussion of assumptions and outcomes. However, it lacks the depth and critical insight required for the top band, making a score of 2 appropriate.

strengths: Relevant differential equations and parameter substitutions are applied effectively to the research question
Mathematics is commensurate with SL level and demonstrates strong understanding of solution techniques
Physical interpretation of mathematical results (damping types, forces) shows thorough conceptual grasp
weaknesses: Minor algebra and unitâconsistency errors appear (e.g., cm vs m, sign in characteristic roots)
Absence of explicit error analysis or uncertainty quantification
Initial conditions and some justifications could be more fully specified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses mathematics effectively and at the appropriate course level, demonstrating thorough understanding and correct application of key techniques. Small errors and lack of error analysis prevent a perfect mark, but the overall standard merits a band-5 score.

strandSelections.reasoning: Derivations are shown step-by-step and linked to physical meaning; graphs are interpreted within the analysis.  Occasional algebra gaps (e.g., sign errors in quadratic solution) and some cluttered sentences slightly reduce clarity, yet overall communication remains clear and logically ordered.

The student demonstrates good understanding of solution techniques and physical interpretation.  Minor derivation errors (signs, unit consistency) appear but do not derail the main results, so accuracy is âmostly correctâ rather than fully or flawlessly correct.

The student employs algebraic derivations, exponential and trigonometric forms, graphs, photographs and a schematic diagram of steer angles.  These representations complement one another and clarify the argument, though additional numerical tables or simulation outputs would have strengthened the evidence for a higher band.

The investigation follows a clear progression: introduction â background theory â derivations â graphical representation â contextual analysis â conclusion.  Although some digressions (e.g., social-media anecdote, repeated explanations of SHM) interrupt the flow, the mathematical argument still develops logically from simple to damped motion and then to real-life implications.  Minor algebra slips and occasional abrupt jumps (such as switching units mid-sentence) prevent it from reaching the exemplary level, but overall coherence is consistently maintained.

Using differential equations, measuring real-world geometry and comparing two safety scenarios displays creativity and initiative, but assumptions remain simplistic (constant b, ignoring rider input).  The investigation is âgoodâ rather than exceptional in its inventive reach.

Results are interpreted in terms of forces, rider capability, and distance to a dead end, showing thoughtful (but not profound) critical analysis.  Mathematical limitations (unit inconsistencies, small-angle simplifications) are acknowledged only briefly, restricting the depth necessary for the top band.

The conclusion summarises findings and the Evaluation section lists weaknesses and proposals for more realistic modelling.  While clear, the discussion remains qualitative; no alternative model or data set is implemented, so evaluation is solid but not comprehensive.

The student reflects on why specific assumptions were adopted and how damping alters the outcome, moving beyond mere description.  Reflection is embedded in Analysis and Evaluation, yet depth is limitedâe.g., no quantitative sensitivity test of parameter choicesâso the work aligns with âmeaningfulâ rather than âsubstantialâ reflection.

Symbols ( x(t), Ï, b, m ) are defined and used consistently in the derivations, and calculus notation is generally correct.  Isolated slips â e.g., writing râ twice instead of râ and râ, or mixing cm with m â break complete accuracy, keeping the work just below the top band.

The narrative is clearly framed around the studentâs own experience and questions (âwhat would my fate have been?â).  Choice of parameters (turning-radius 31 cm, measured road length) shows a clear personal approach, yet the modelling framework itself remains conventional, placing the work in the middle band.

Most material is relevant to damping and speed wobble, yet lengthy narrative passages, repeated definitions, and overly detailed screen-captured Desmos links add bulk without advancing the mathematics.  Trimming these would not harm the argument, showing that conciseness is only âmostlyâ achieved rather than fully.

Differential-equation solutions for damped motion, parameter substitutions, and velocity/force calculations are directly relevant to the research question and are applied correctly in most places.  Occasional algebra/notation slips and reliance on secondary parameter estimates limit the work from becoming âcomprehensiveâ, hence an âeffectiveâ level of relevance.

Selecting a personal cycling incident and determining path length, mass and damping data demonstrate genuine independent thinking beyond textbook replicas.  However, most of the mathematics closely follows standard SHM/damping derivations with little novel adaptation, so evidence is significant but not outstanding.

Sections are headed systematically (Introduction, Assumptions, Background, Modelling, Analysis, Conclusion, Evaluation).  Graphs and figures are embedded near the text that references them, aiding navigation.  Some headings are duplicated (two âBackgroundâ subsections) and equation numbering is absent, so internal cross-referencing is weak, but the reader can still follow the structure without difficulty.

Content (second-order ODEs, complex roots, exponential envelopes) is squarely within and slightly beyond AA-HL expectations, showing full commensurateness with course level.  However, numerical force calculations are simplistic, and error analysis is absent, preventing the âwell-appliedâ (5) designation.

annotations.comment: The classification of damping types is accurate; consider giving a brief real-world example for each case to deepen understanding.
The calculation of \(W\) includes a subtraction inside the square root; check the algebraic manipulation for the term \(36/17956\) to ensure accuracy.
The parameter \(b\) is introduced with units \(\mathrm{Nm\,s/rad}\); clarify how this relates to the linear damping coefficient in your ODE to avoid confusion.
The title page clearly indicates the subject and level; consider adding your name and candidate details for completeness.
Good concise indication of the assessment type; ensure any administrative information follows IB guidelines.
Strong personal engagement demonstrated through the anecdote connecting to your own experience, which frames the investigation effectively.
The definition of damping is included here; consider summarizing or moving background theory to its own section to maintain focus in the introduction.
Aim 1 restates modelling without damping; ensure each aim is measurable and distinct to improve clarity and reduce redundancy.
The assumption excluding rider control is clearly stated, but discuss how removing rider input impacts real-world applicability.
The background description is thorough, though consider breaking long paragraphs into smaller chunks to improve readability and clarity.
Figure 1 effectively illustrates speed wobble, but adding labels to the diagramâs axes would enhance comprehension of forces.
The SHO formula $$F=-kx$$ is correctly stated; include equation numbering to facilitate cross-referencing in the text.
The transition to the damped oscillator section is logical; consider renaming duplicate 'Background' headers to maintain consistent structure.
The definition of \(v\) as velocity is clear; specify that this is angular or linear velocity to avoid ambiguity.
The derivation starts by equating the net force correctly; improve communication by aligning equals signs in multiline equations.
The characteristic equation is derived systematically; however, note the sign in $$r=\pm i\sqrt{\tfrac{k}{m}}$$ requires clearer justification.
The general solution $$x(t)=A_1 e^{i\omega t}+A_2 e^{-i\omega t}$$ is correct; consider explaining why \(A_1\) and \(A_2\) can be complex for clarity.
Eulerâs identity is applied properly, but the step combining terms could benefit from an explicit intermediate step for transparency.
You measure amplitude in cm but mix units later; convert 31 cm to meters consistently to avoid unit inconsistency.
Figure 2 supports the SHM graph, yet include axis labels and units on the image to enhance mathematical clarity.
Calculation of $$\omega$$ from frequency is correct; explicitly state units for $$\omega$$ (rad/s) to improve mathematical precision.
Referencing Wikipedia for fundamental constants lacks rigor; prioritize peer-reviewed or primary sources for more reliable data.
Graph 1 caption is descriptive; include the equation used to generate the plot for reproducibility.
The damped motion differential equation is correctly formulated; specify initial conditions to fully define the solution.
You list two expressions for \(r\) but repeat \(r_1\); correct the second to \(r_2\) to maintain notation consistency.
